You were but a child when you left, and I did not know then how much of my heart would be left behind. I have tried to write to you, but the sea has been unkind, and the ink has often run from my quill before I can form a sentence. The postman has grown weary of my letters, and I fear the storm may come before my next message reaches you.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I must confess that I have not been able to write to you in quite some time. Five years, to be precise. It is a strange thing, this silence between us. I have often wondered if you would ever forgive me for the years I have been absent, or if you have moved on without me. But tonight, as I sit in the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, I find myself compelled to speak to you once more.

The storm is coming, and it is a most formidable one. The sea, which has always been both my companion and my torment, roars with a fury that I have never known. **My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit by the flickering light of the oil lamp, its glow casting long shadows upon the walls of this lonely tower. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would devour the very earth. I write this with trembling hands, for I fear the storm may claim me before dawn, and I wish to leave something behind—something that might reach you, should I not return.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the ache of your absence lingers like the scent of salt upon the air. I have not spoken to you, nor have I written, for I feared what words might bring. But now, as the heavens rage and the waves threaten to swallow the lighthouse whole, I find myself compelled to speak, to confess, to beg.

You were but a child when you ran from our home, and I do not blame you. I was a fool, a man too hardened by years of solitude, too blind to see the fire in your eyes. You were not the one who turned away, Eliza. It was I, who let the world grow cold between us.

I have spent these years watching the sea, watching the storm, watching the light burn bright against the dark. And in that light, I have seen the reflection of you—your laughter, your sorrow, your longing. I have been a fool, but I have not forgotten you.

**Whose Name I Have Not Uttered in Five Years,**

**I Write This Letter in the Trembling Light of the Lighthouse,**

**As the Storm That Shall Unmake the World Draws Near.**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

**Thy Mother’s Heart, Though Silent, Still Beats for Thee,**

**Even as the Waves Shall Claim the Rock and the Light.**

---

**I Write This With Ink That Dries to Dust,**

**And Thou, My Child, Was Right.**

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading these words, then you are alive, and I am writing them with trembling hands and a heart that has grown heavier with each passing year. I have not seen you in five, and the silence between us has grown like a storm, fierce and unrelenting, though it is not the kind that breaks the sea—it is the kind that cracks the soul.

I have been here, in this tower, for many years now. You know, I have always been a man of routine, of order, of the sea. The lighthouse has been my home, my sanctuary, and for a long time, I thought it was the only place I would ever truly belong. But lately... lately, the sea has been restless, and the wind has whispered secrets I cannot unhear. And tonight, my dear, the storm comes.

You do not know how much I have thought of you. How much I have missed you. I have not spoken of you in years, not because I have forgotten you, but because I feared the words would not be enough. I feared they would not be true. I feared they would not be enough to mend what has been broken. And yet, as the wind howls and the waves crash, I find myself thinking of your laughter, of your voice, of the way you used to run up the stairs with your books in hand, as if they were treasures more precious than the stars above.

I have been here, alone, for so long, and the loneliness has grown into something that gnaws at my very soul.

I write to you now, with the ink still wet on the page and the sea howling at my window, for I have not slept since the storm began to gather. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1883**

**Lighthouse, Northumberland**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heart heavy with sorrow and a mind clouded by the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world may have turned, the ache of absence remains ever fresh in my soul.

You were once my pride, my light in the darkness of this lonely life. I have watched the tides and the stars, the winds and the storms, and yet none have ever stirred the depths of my heart as the memory of you. I have seen the dawn rise and the dusk fall, and still, the echoes of your laughter and your words remain as vivid as the first day we spoke.

But now, my dear Eleanor, the storm is coming. The winds howl like the wails of a ghost, and the waves lash against the rocks with fury. The sky is black as the night of a soul bereft of hope. I have seen the tempest approach with a dread that none can name. I have no doubt that this night shall be the last of my days, and I write this with the knowledge that I may not see the dawn again.

And yet, I cannot help but think of you. I have no words to offer you, no comfort to give, but I must speak, for I have no other voice to call upon.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know not a single word I write will reach you. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown as vast and unyielding as the sea. Yet, as I sit here in this lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, I find myself compelled to write, not for you, but for myself. For the first time in years, I feel the weight of this silence pressing down upon me, and I must speak, even if it is only to the wind.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the nights you would come to the lighthouse as a child, with your small hands clutching my coat, and your eyes wide with wonder as you watched the sea and the stars. You would ask me questions about the lantern, about the sea, about the stars, and I would tell you stories, though I never knew if you listened or not. You were so curious, so full of questions, and I would answer them all, even if I did not know the answers myself.
